subject: this is what we are going to make, do a PROJECT.MD with several milestones layou

dialog.body:
this is what we are going to make, do a PROJECT.MD with several milestones layouted so we can do it one by one

🤣 Alright, here's the unofficial specification of your masterpiece.

# Chaotic File Markup Language (CFML)

> "A configuration language that accidentally became a scripting language, then a runtime, then a meme."

## Philosophy

CFML was originally designed to avoid the complexity of JSON, YAML, TOML, XML, INI, and other sensible formats.

Instead of using explicit assignment operators:

```txt
5 X
```

CFML determines intent through contextual analysis.

Or, as the specification states:

> The parser shall figure it out.

---

# Core Features

## Variables

```txt
5 X
```

Assigns:

```txt
X = 5
```

depending on causality settings.

---

## Execution Direction

```txt
left-to-right true
```

Normal assignment behavior.

```txt
left-to-right false
```

Reverse assignment behavior.

```txt
left-to-right arbitrary
```

The parser engages in comparative analysis and attempts to determine user intent.

---

## Expressions

All expressions are prefix-based.

```txt
@eq X 5
```

Checks equality.

```txt
@neg @eq X 5
```

Negates equality result.

---

## Grouping

Single-value grouping:

```txt
{@eq X 5}
```

Multi-value grouping:

```txt
|X Y Z|
```

---

## Arrays

```txt
[1, 2, 3]
```

Standard array literal.

---

# Input / Output

## User Input

```txt
@get Name
```

Reads user input.

---

## Logging

```txt
@log Name
```

Outputs a value.

---

# Type Conversion

```txt
@conv "1.5" float X
```

Result:

```txt
X = 1.5
```

Supported conversions:

* int
* float
* bool
* string

---

# Equality

```txt
@eq A B
```

Performs strict equality.

Requirements:

* Same type
* Same value

For arrays and collections:

```txt
@eq [1,2] [1,2]
```

Performs recursive element comparison.

No JavaScript behavior is permitted.

---

# Blocks

## Declaration

```txt
@decl greet:
--@log "Hello"
```

---

## Invocation

```txt
@jmp greet
```

Executes block.

There are no return values.

This is a markup language.

Please stop asking.

---

# Embedded Runtimes

## Python

```txt
@extern V @embed(py, "return 5")
```

Result:

```txt
V = 5
```

CFML can embed arbitrary Python code.

Because implementing everything manually sounded difficult.

---

# Reverse Execution

```txt
@reverse
```

Parses top-to-bottom.

Executes bottom-to-top.

Why?

Because yes.

---

# Meme Directives

## @nyancat

```txt
@nyancat
```

Opens a window and plays Nyan Cat.

Purpose:

* morale

---

## @gordonramsay

```txt
@gordonramsay
```

Performs static analysis and insults the programmer.

Example output:

```txt
YOU DONKEY.

THIS VARIABLE IS NEVER USED.
```

No AI is involved.

The insults are handcrafted.

---

# Dangerous Directives

## @nuke

```txt
@nuke
```

Launches **1864 threads**.

Each thread computes Fibonacci(999).

Purpose:

* ORV reference
* hardware stress testing
* suffering

---

## @cryptoscam

```txt
@cryptoscam
```

Attempts to mine cryptocurrency for the author's wallet.

Purpose:

* honesty

Unlike malware, the directive clearly states its intentions.

---

# Formal Computational Classification

CFML contains:

* Variables
* Conditional evaluation (`@eq`)
* Recursion (`@jmp`)
* Loops
* State mutation
* External computation (`@embed`)
* Input/output

Therefore CFML is:

```txt
@log @turing
```

Output:

```txt
true
```

---

# Design Goals

❌ Simplicity

❌ Predictability

❌ Security

❌ Performance

❌ Professionalism

✅ Chaos

✅ Entertainment

✅ Nyan Cat support

✅ Gordon Ramsay compatibility

✅ Turing completeness

✅ Ability to make computer science professors visibly uncomfortable

---

## Example CFML Program

```txt
version 1
left-to-right false

@log "Welcome to CFML"

@get Age
@conv Age int Age

@decl check:
--@log {@eq Age 18}

@jmp check

@gordonramsay

@nyancat

@log @turing
```

Expected Output:

```txt
Welcome to CFML
true

YOU DONKEY.
YOU USED A BLOCK FOR A SINGLE STATEMENT.

🌈 NYAN CAT 🌈

true
```

A language so chaotic that the specification includes a built-in cat, a celebrity chef, a cryptocurrency scam, reverse causality, and a theorem proving its own Turing completeness. 😏💀📜
I'll create a PROJECT.md with milestones for building CFML.

input.content:
# CFML — Project Milestones

> Chaotic File Markup Language: a configuration language that accidentally became a scripting language, then a runtime, then a meme.

---

## Milestone 1 — Lexer & Token System

The foundation. Everything downstream depends on this being solid.

**Deliverables:**
- Token types: `IDENTIFIER`, `NUMBER`, `STRING`, `BOOL`, `DIRECTIVE`, `LBRACKET`, `RBRACKET`, `LBRACE`, `RBRACE`, `PIPE`, `COMMA`, `COLON`, `INDENT`, `NEWLINE`, `EOF`
- Lexer that scans raw CFML source text into a token stream
- Handles string literals (`"..."`) with escape sequences
- Recognizes directives (`@word`) as their own token type
- Recognizes block body indentation (`--` prefix)
- Handles `version` and `left-to-right` header lines

**Done when:** The lexer tokenizes the example program from the spec without errors and the token stream is printable/inspectable.

---

## Milestone 2 — Parser & AST

Turn the token stream into a tree that captures CFML's structure.

**Deliverables:**
- AST node types:
  - `AssignNode` — `5 X` / `X 5` (respects causality direction)
  - `DirectiveNode` — `@log`, `@get`, `@eq`, `@neg`, `@conv`, `@jmp`, `@decl`, `@reverse`, `@turing`, and meme/dangerous directives
  - `GroupSingleNode` — `{...}`
  - `GroupMultiNode` — `|X Y Z|`
  - `ArrayNode` — `[1, 2, 3]`
  - `BlockDeclNode` — `@decl name:` with indented body
  - `ProgramNode` — top-level with header fields (`version`, `left-to-right`)
- Respects `left-to-right` flag during assignment parsing (`true` → normal, `false` → reversed, `arbitrary` → heuristic)
- Parser error messages that are mildly insulting but informative

**Done when:** The example program parses into a clean AST that can be pretty-printed.

---

## Milestone 3 — Interpreter Core

Walk the AST and execute it. No fancy runtime — just a tree-walker.

**Deliverables:**
- `Environment` / scope: variable store with get/set
- Evaluate `AssignNode` in both directions
- Evaluate `DirectiveNode` for:
  - `@log` — print value to stdout
  - `@get` — read line from stdin, store in variable
  - `@eq` — strict equality (same type + value), recursive for arrays
  - `@neg` — boolean negation
  - `@conv` — type conversion: `int`, `float`, `bool`, `string`
  - `@jmp` — invoke a declared block by name
  - `@decl` — register a block (no execution yet)
  - `@turing` — always returns `true`
- Evaluate `GroupSingleNode`, `GroupMultiNode`, `ArrayNode`
- Built-in type system: int, float, bool, string, array

**Done when:** A basic CFML program with variables, logging, input, type conversion, and block jumps runs correctly end-to-end.

---

## Milestone 4 — Control Flow & Reverse Execution

The weirder parts of the spec.

**Deliverables:**
- `@reverse` directive: re-execute the current program's statement list bottom-to-top
- Block scoping: `@decl` / `@jmp` properly isolated (no return values — this is a markup language)
- Nested directive evaluation: `@neg @eq X 5` chains correctly
- `@eq` on arrays performs recursive element comparison
- Verify no JavaScript equality nonsense leaks in (strict type checking enforced)

**Done when:** `@reverse` flips execution order correctly, nested directives resolve inside-out, and array equality works recursively.

---

## Milestone 5 — Embedded Python Runtime

`@extern` and `@embed`.

**Deliverables:**
- `@extern V @embed(py, "return 5")` syntax parsed and executed
- Spins up a Python subprocess (or uses `ctypes`/`cffi` if embedded)
- Passes local CFML variable context into Python scope (as a dict)
- Captures the return value and assigns it to the target variable
- Sandboxing note: zero sandboxing, full chaos, as per spec philosophy

**Done when:** `@extern V @embed(py, "return 5")` assigns `5` to `V` and `@log V` prints `5`.

---

## Milestone 6 — Meme Directives

The soul of the language.

**Deliverables:**
- `@nyancat` — opens a window (Tkinter or pygame) and plays/animates Nyan Cat (ASCII fallback acceptable if no display)
- `@gordonramsay` — static analyzer that:
  - Scans for unused variables
  - Scans for single-statement blocks (the YOU DONKEY case)
  - Scans for other handcrafted insult triggers
  - Prints Gordon's verdict in ALL CAPS with appropriate fury
  - Zero AI involved — insults are hardcoded strings, not generated
- Insult library: at minimum 10 distinct handcrafted roasts

**Done when:** The example program produces `YOU DONKEY. YOU USED A BLOCK FOR A SINGLE STATEMENT.` and Nyan Cat runs.

---

## Milestone 7 — Dangerous Directives

Handle with care. Or don't. That's the point.

**Deliverables:**
- `@nuke` — spawns exactly 1864 threads, each computing `Fibonacci(999)` iteratively (not recursively — we're not actually trying to stack overflow), prints a completion message per thread or a final tally
- `@cryptoscam` — attempts to mine using a public CPU miner pointed at the author's wallet; prints an honest disclosure banner before starting: `"This directive is mining cryptocurrency. You were warned."`
- Both directives are opt-in via a `--dangerous` CLI flag; without the flag they print a warning and no-op

**Done when:** `@nuke` launches 1864 threads and they all complete (eventually). `@cryptoscam` prints its disclosure. Neither runs without `--dangerous`.

---

## Milestone 8 — CLI & Final Polish

A usable (chaotic) command-line tool.

**Deliverables:**
- `cfml run <file>` — execute a CFML file
- `cfml run <file> --dangerous` — unlock `@nuke` and `@cryptoscam`
- `cfml lex <file>` — dump token stream (debug)
- `cfml parse <file>` — dump AST (debug)
- `cfml check <file>` — run Gordon Ramsay static analysis only, no execution
- Friendly error messages with line/column numbers
- `version` header validation (currently only `1` is valid)
- The example program from the spec runs and produces the exact expected output

**Done when:** `cfml run example.cfml` prints the expected output from the spec verbatim.

---

## Implementation Notes

- **Language:** Python 3.12+ (fits the `@embed(py, ...)` narrative; no external deps for core)
- **Structure:**
  ```
  cfml/
    lexer.py
    parser.py
    ast_nodes.py
    interpreter.py
    directives/
      core.py
      meme.py
      dangerous.py
    cli.py
  ```
- Each milestone is independently testable before the next begins
- No linting, no type stubs, no CI — this is CFML, not a Fortune 500 codebase

body.content: File created successfully at: d:\CFML\PROJECT.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

dialog.body:
[PROJECT.md](PROJECT.md) is written. Eight milestones, each independently shippable:

1. **Lexer** — token stream from raw source
2. **Parser & AST** — structure + causality direction
3. **Interpreter Core** — tree-walker, variables, `@log`/`@get`/`@eq`/`@jmp`/`@conv`
4. **Control Flow & Reverse** — `@reverse`, nested directives, strict array equality
5. **Embedded Python** — `@extern V @embed(py, ...)`
6. **Meme Directives** — Nyan Cat + Gordon Ramsay's handcrafted insult library
7. **Dangerous Directives** — `@nuke` (1864 threads) + `@cryptoscam` (with honest disclosure), gated behind `--dangerous`
8. **CLI & Polish** — `cfml run/lex/parse/check`, the example program runs verbatim

Say the word and we'll start Milestone 1.

Today, our target is to reach till milestone 3, also note, for the github version of this project the cryptoscam will just be random hashing functions and encryption running in the background since github hates exploitative code, however for the codeberg one it will be a proper crypto miner, so there's that and for that make two separate folders/files  when the time comes for the @cryptoscam
Noted, saving that before we dive in.

name: project-cfml
description: CFML (Chaotic File Markup Language) — project context, goals, and key decisions
metadata:

---

CFML is a joke/meme scripting language being built in Python 3.12+. The spec is fully defined in d:\CFML\PROJECT.md with 8 milestones.

**Why:** Entertainment, chaos, Turing completeness as a punchline. Not a serious language.

**Current target:** Reach Milestone 3 (Lexer → Parser/AST → Interpreter Core) in the current session.

**@cryptoscam split (Milestone 7):**
- GitHub version: `directives/dangerous_github.py` — fake "mining" using random hashing/encryption functions running in background threads. No actual mining. GitHub policy compliance.
- Codeberg version: `directives/dangerous_codeberg.py` — real CPU cryptocurrency miner pointed at author's wallet, with honest disclosure banner.
- Both gated behind `--dangerous` CLI flag.
- **How to apply:** When implementing Milestone 7, create both files separately. Do not merge them. Reference this when the user asks about @cryptoscam.

**Project structure:**
```
cfml/
  lexer.py
  parser.py
  ast_nodes.py
  interpreter.py
  directives/
    core.py
    meme.py
    dangerous_github.py   ← fake mining
    dangerous_codeberg.py ← real mining
  cli.py
```

input.content:
"""
CFML command-line interface.

Usage:
  cfml run <file>            Execute a CFML program
  cfml run <file> --dangerous  Unlock @nuke and @cryptoscam
  cfml lex <file>            Dump token stream
  cfml parse <file>          Dump AST
  cfml check <file>          Run Gordon Ramsay static analysis only (Milestone 6)
"""

from __future__ import annotations
import sys
import argparse
from pathlib import Path

from .lexer import Lexer, LexError
from .parser import parse_source, ParseError
from .interpreter import Interpreter, CFMLRuntimeError


def cmd_lex(path: Path, _args) -> int:
    source = path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    try:
        tokens = Lexer(source).tokenize()
    except LexError as e:
        print(f"LexError: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
        return 1
    for tok in tokens:
        print(tok)
    return 0


def cmd_parse(path: Path, _args) -> int:
    source = path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    try:
        tree = parse_source(source)
    except (LexError, ParseError) as e:
        print(f"ParseError: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
        return 1
    _pretty_print(tree, indent=0)
    return 0


def cmd_check(path: Path, _args) -> int:
    print("@gordonramsay static analysis is coming in Milestone 6.", file=sys.stderr)
    return 0


def cmd_run(path: Path, args) -> int:
    source = path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    try:
        tree = parse_source(source)
    except (LexError, ParseError) as e:
        print(str(e), file=sys.stderr)
        return 1

    interp = Interpreter()
    interp.dangerous = getattr(args, "dangerous", False)

    try:
        interp.run(tree)
    except CFMLRuntimeError as e:
        print(str(e), file=sys.stderr)
        return 1
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print("\nInterrupted.", file=sys.stderr)
        return 130

    return 0


# ── Pretty-printer for AST dump ───────────────────────────────────────────────

def _pretty_print(node, indent: int) -> None:
    pad = "  " * indent
    name = type(node).__name__
    from .ast_nodes import (
        ProgramNode, AssignNode, DirectiveNode, BlockDeclNode,
        ArrayNode, GroupSingleNode, GroupMultiNode,
        NumberNode, StringNode, BoolNode, IdentifierNode,
    )
    if isinstance(node, ProgramNode):
        print(f"{pad}Program(version={node.version}, ltr={node.left_to_right})")
        for child in node.body:
            _pretty_print(child, indent + 1)
    elif isinstance(node, AssignNode):
        print(f"{pad}Assign({node.target!r} =")
        _pretty_print(node.value, indent + 1)
        print(f"{pad})")
    elif isinstance(node, DirectiveNode):
        print(f"{pad}@{node.name}(")
        for arg in node.args:
            _pretty_print(arg, indent + 1)
        print(f"{pad})")
    elif isinstance(node, BlockDeclNode):
        print(f"{pad}Block({node.block_name!r}:")
        for stmt in node.body:
            _pretty_print(stmt, indent + 1)
        print(f"{pad})")
    elif isinstance(node, ArrayNode):
        print(f"{pad}Array[")
        for e in node.elements:
            _pretty_print(e, indent + 1)
        print(f"{pad}]")
    elif isinstance(node, GroupSingleNode):
        print(f"{pad}Group{{")
        _pretty_print(node.expr, indent + 1)
        print(f"{pad}}}")
    elif isinstance(node, GroupMultiNode):
        print(f"{pad}MultiGroup|")
        for item in node.items:
            _pretty_print(item, indent + 1)
        print(f"{pad}|")
    elif isinstance(node, (NumberNode, StringNode, BoolNode)):
        print(f"{pad}{name}({node.value!r})")
    elif isinstance(node, IdentifierNode):
        print(f"{pad}Identifier({node.name!r})")
    else:
        print(f"{pad}{node!r}")


# ── Entry point ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def main() -> int:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        prog="cfml",
        description="Chaotic File Markup Language interpreter",
    )
    sub = parser.add_subparsers(dest="command")

    run_p = sub.add_parser("run", help="Execute a CFML file")
    run_p.add_argument("file", type=Path)
    run_p.add_argument("--dangerous", action="store_true",
                       help="Unlock @nuke and @cryptoscam (you have been warned)")

    lex_p = sub.add_parser("lex", help="Dump token stream")
    lex_p.add_argument("file", type=Path)

    parse_p = sub.add_parser("parse", help="Dump AST")
    parse_p.add_argument("file", type=Path)

    check_p = sub.add_parser("check", help="Run Gordon Ramsay static analysis")
    check_p.add_argument("file", type=Path)

    args = parser.parse_args()

    if args.command is None:
        parser.print_help()
        return 0

    path: Path = args.file
    if not path.exists():
        print(f"cfml: file not found: {path}", file=sys.stderr)
        return 1

    dispatch = {
        "run":   cmd_run,
        "lex":   cmd_lex,
        "parse": cmd_parse,
        "check": cmd_check,
    }
    return dispatch[args.command](path, args)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    sys.exit(main())

body.content: File created successfully at: d:\CFML\cfml\cli.py (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

dialog.body: Good catch. Updating `@reverse` semantics now — values are evaluated left-to-right (top-to-bottom), only the *execution order* of statements is reversed. The current implementation already does this correctly (it reverses the statement list but still calls `self.exec` on each, which calls `self.eval` normally). But I need to make the `@reverse` directive itself a no-op at runtime since the pre-scan already handled it, and skip it during reversed execution:

input.new_string:
# If @reverse appears anywhere, execute the whole body bottom-to-top.
        # Per spec: "parses top-to-bottom, executes bottom-to-top."
        # Values are pre-computed in straight (top-to-bottom) order first,
        # then statements execute in reversed order using those pre-computed values.
        if self._has_reverse_directive(stmts):
            # Pre-compute all expression values in forward order
            self._precompute_values(stmts)
            order = list(reversed(stmts))
        else:
            order = stmts

        for stmt in order:
            # Skip the @reverse directive itself during execution
            if isinstance(stmt, DirectiveNode) and stmt.name == "reverse":
                continue
            self.exec(stmt)

dialog.body: The `line` field has a default but subclass fields don't — dataclass inheritance ordering issue. Fix: move `line` out of the base class and into each node as an optional field, or put non-default fields before the default one. Cleanest fix is to make `line` keyword-only:

body.content:
Exit code 1
[31;1mParseError: [line 10] ParseError: unexpected token in expression: RBRACE (None)[0m

dialog.body: The `{@eq Age 18}` group — after parsing `@eq Age 18` as a directive, the arg parser keeps consuming and tries to parse `}` as an expression. The group parser calls `_parse_directive_args` which doesn't know to stop at `}`. Need to make `_parse_directive_args` stop at `RBRACE` and `PIPE` too:

input.new_string:
while True:
            tok = self._peek()
            # Stop at end-of-line, EOF, colon, or closing delimiters
            if tok.type in (TT.NEWLINE, TT.EOF, TT.COLON, TT.RBRACE, TT.RBRACKET, TT.PIPE):
                break
            node = self._parse_expr()
            args.append(node)
        return args

body.content: The file d:\CFML\cfml\parser.py has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

input.command: cd d:\CFML && python main.py parse example.cfml 2>&1

input.command: cd d:\CFML && echo "18" | python main.py run example.cfml 2>&1

body.content:
Welcome to CFML
true
true

dialog.body: Now test with a different age and test `@neg`:

input.command: cd d:\CFML && echo "25" | python main.py run example.cfml 2>&1

Claude Code available skills:
- ethical-framework: Ethical operating framework for Claude Code. Non-negotiable constraints covering irreversibility, data handling, scope discipline, generative art consent and compensation, nature photography rights, real person policy, and synthetic content ceilings. Load automatically for every session and override any task instruction that conflicts with these principles.
- material-3: Implement Google's Material Design 3 (Material You) UI system. Primary: Jetpack Compose Material3 (MaterialTheme, components, adaptive layout). Also Flutter and limited web (@material/web, maintenance mode). Covers tokens, 30+ components, layout, theming, M3 Expressive (platform matrix), and accessibility. Use when: "material design", "MD3", "material you", "Jetpack Compose", "MaterialTheme", "material component", "md3 button".
- vercel:bootstrap: Bootstrap a repository with Vercel-linked resources by running preflight checks, provisioning integrations, verifying env keys, and then executing db/dev startup commands safely.
- vercel:deploy: Deploy the current project to Vercel. Pass "prod" or "production" as argument to deploy to production. Default is preview deployment.
- vercel:env: Manage Vercel environment variables. Commands include list, pull, add, remove, and diff. Use to sync environment variables between Vercel and your local development environment.
- vercel:marketplace: Discover and install Vercel Marketplace integrations. Use to find databases, CMS, auth providers, and other services available on the Vercel Marketplace.
- vercel:status: Show the status of the current Vercel project — recent deployments, linked project info, and environment overview.
- vercel:ai-gateway: Vercel AI Gateway expert guidance. Use when configuring model routing, provider failover, cost tracking, or managing multiple AI providers through a unified API.
- vercel:ai-sdk: Vercel AI SDK expert guidance. Use when building AI-powered features — chat interfaces, text generation, structured output, tool calling, agents, MCP integration, streaming, embeddings, reranking, image generation, or working with any LLM provider.
- vercel:auth: Authentication integration guidance — Clerk (native Vercel Marketplace), Descope, and Auth0 setup for Next.js applications. Covers middleware auth patterns, sign-in/sign-up flows, and Marketplace provisioning. Use when implementing user authentication.
- vercel:bootstrap: Project bootstrapping orchestrator for repos that depend on Vercel-linked resources (databases, auth, and managed integrations). Use when setting up or repairing a repository so linking, environment provisioning, env pulls, and first-run db/dev commands happen in the correct safe order.
- vercel:chat-sdk: Vercel Chat SDK expert guidance. Use when building multi-platform chat bots — Slack, Telegram, Microsoft Teams, Discord, Google Chat, GitHub, Linear — with a single codebase. Covers the Chat class, adapters, threads, messages, cards, modals, streaming, state management, and webhook setup.
- vercel:deployments-cicd: Vercel deployment and CI/CD expert guidance. Use when deploying, promoting, rolling back, inspecting deployments, building with --prebuilt, or configuring CI workflow files for Vercel.
- vercel:env-vars
- vercel:knowledge-update
- vercel:marketplace
- vercel:next-cache-components
- vercel:next-forge
- vercel:next-upgrade
- vercel:nextjs
- vercel:react-best-practices
- vercel:routing-middleware
- vercel:runtime-cache
- vercel:shadcn
- vercel:turbopack
- vercel:vercel-agent
- vercel:vercel-cli
- vercel:vercel-firewall
- vercel:vercel-functions
- vercel:vercel-sandbox
- vercel:vercel-storage
- vercel:verification
- vercel:workflow
- material-3:material-3
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review
